Tactile Testing {#S2-3} --------------- Tactile stimuli were delivered to two fingers with a custom-built tactile stimulator (Cortical Metrics, Carrboro, NC, USA). Control participants underwent a battery of testing conducted on the index and middle fingers of both right and left hands. Upper extremity amputees completed the testing with *the intact hand*. Testing included temporal order judgment and duration discrimination tasks. During the testing session, the participants were situated with their arm (right then left for controls, intact arm for amputees) on a wrist support and fingers positioned appropriately over the tactile stimulator. Mechanical stimulation was applied to the tips of the index and middle fingers. A computerized procedure guided participants through a series of questions, answered *via* verbal report and recorded by a research member, relating to what the participants perceived on the tips of each finger. In both of the tasks described below, a simple tracking paradigm was used to determine each participant's difference limen, the amount that the stimulus must be changed in order for differences between finger perceptions to be detected. Visual cues on the computer screen informed participants about appropriate times to provide their response. Powered by its included aAA NiMH rechargeable battery, Titan Plus can run for up to seven hours before it needs a charge. Or, for added convenience when recharging isn’t an option, it can be powered by a single disposable AAA alkaline, readily available worldwide. Activating Titan Plus is as easy as twisting its head switch counterclockwise for low output. For medium output, simply return to off by rotating the switch clockwise, then counterclockwise again within one second. Repeat the process a third time to unleash all 300 lumens from its microprocessor-controlled LED. For sheer versatility, portability, durability, and functionality, the compact and lightweight Titan Plus packs a serious punch. This rugged, go-anywhere keychain flashlight belongs in the pocket (pant or shirt) or purse of anyone who wants to be ready for anything, anytime, anywhere. It’ll turn heads in more ways than one. texts.full_text: hree years. This will be paid in monthly increments and is aimed at reducing peak-hour congestion on roads by encouraging more people to use buses instead of buses. The increased subsidies come as part of the Government's wider strategy for improving public transport infrastructure, with a particular emphasis being placed on commuter rail services that link major cities across England such as London or Manchester. The strategic approach aims not only at reducing congestion but also providing an environmentally sustainable alternative option to road travel which has become increasingly popular in recent years. In addition the deal includes measures aimed towards improving passenger experience on South Central trains, with plans for investment into new carriages and improved journey times between major stations such as Waterloo (London), Brighton or Southampton. This will include upgrades including faster services during peak hours along existing routes to make rail travel more attractive than road transport. The Strategic Rail Authority has also committed resources towards developing the rail network in areas that are currently underutilized, with a view of increasing service frequency and capacity on these lines over time as demand grows or becomes apparent. The goal is for South Central trains eventually become an integral part not just passengers' daily journeys but leisure activities across southern England. The increased subsidies represent significant investment into the rail sector by Government funds that are expected to pay dividends in terms of economic growth and improved quality-of-life benefits, including reduced congestion on roads which can lead directly or indirectly cause pollution. The financial support also demonstrates confidence from public authorities towards South Central Rail's ability as a leading transport provider. The deal with Strategic Rail Authority is the latest step by Government bodies aiming at improving British rail services through strategic investments and policy reforms that encourage greater use of trains for daily commutes, thereby reducing traffic congestion on roads. The goal remains to provide an efficient public transport system in line with national environmental targets while also stimulating economic growth across regional markets.

This excerpt from the ballet score, Cinderella is one example: [unordered_list style=”tick”] Find on iTunes Find at Amazon [/unordered_list] Ives: Symphony No. 2 The final movement of Charles Ives’ Symphony No. 2 is an exuberant collage of American folk songs, hymns, and Civil War military songs. You might also hear hints of Dvorak’s “New World” Symphony. The end of the movement is like the grand finale of a brilliant fireworks display. Listen carefully. Something happens on the final chord… [unordered_list style=”tick”] Find this recording on iTunes Find at Amazon [/unordered_list] Shostakovich: Polka from “The Golden Age” Dmitri Shostakovich’s 1930 ballet The Golden Age offered a satirical look at cultural and political currents in 1920s Europe. The Polka lands somewhere between humor and sarcasm: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0z4MfzSdNkI [unordered_list style=”tick”] Find on iTunes Find at Amazon [/unordered_list] Schnittke: Stille Nacht Russian composer Alfred Schnittke (1934-1998) wrote this haunting version of Silent Night as a musical Christmas Card for violinist Gidon Kremer in 1978. Schnittke spent much of his life trapped behind the Soviet Iron Curtain. His music often evokes an atmosphere of gloom as well as biting protest. Pastiche and historical references frequently make up the ironic fabric of Schnittke’s music. Here's how you could implement this: ### Step 1: Set Up Your Versioning Firstly, ensure that your project properties in Visual Studio are correctly set to auto-increment the version number whenever you publish it. - Go to `Project` > `[Your Project Name] Properties`. - Under the `Application` tab, find the `Assembly Information` button and click on it. - Ensure "Version" is something like 1.0.* which allows for automatic build numbers and revision updates upon each compile/publish operation. ### Step 2: Access Version at Runtime You can then use reflection in your C# code to read this version information from the assembly attributes: ```csharp using System; using System.Reflection; public class Program { [STAThread] static void Main() { Application.EnableVisualStyles(); Application.SetCompatibleTextRenderingDefault(false); var mainForm = new MainForm(); // Assuming your form is named MainForm. mainForm.Show(); Application.Run(mainForm); string versionInfo = GetAssemblyVersion(); MessageBox.Show($"Current Version: {versionInfo}"); } private static string GetAssemblyVersion() { Assembly assembly = Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly(); object[] attributes = assembly.GetCustomAttributes(typeof(AssemblyInformationalVersionAttribute), false); if (attributes.Length > 0) return ((AssemblyInformationalVersionAttribute)attributes[0]).InformationalVersion; Version version = assembly.GetName().Version; // Default fallback to display Major.Minor.Build.Revision return $"{version.Major}.{version.Minor}.{version.Build}.{version.Revision}"; } } ``` ### Step 3: Display in a TextBox You can set this version information directly into your `TextBox` within the form's load event or constructor: ```csharp public partial class MainForm : Form { public MainForm() { InitializeComponent(); // Assuming you have a TextBox named 'versionTextBox' string versionInfo = Program.GetAssemblyVersion(); versionTextBox.Text = $"Current Version: {versionInfo}"; } } ``` ### Notes - Debug vs. Release: Ensure that the application runs in release mode when testing this, as debug builds may not use the assembly's full build process. - Network Deployment Consideration: If your app is network deployed or requires specific deployment settings (e.g., ClickOnce), make sure these are configured properly to reflect changes. texts.full_text: e situation there has developed in a way that does not coincide with our philosophy of authenticity, as the sateré-Mawé there had become increasingly touristy. So when we were asked by the editor and host of the German TV Series “Galileo” if we could help them to film the rites of the bullet ant dances, we looked at the roots of that tribe and found them at the Marau river in the municipality of Maués, about 1 hour by private air charter from Manaus. 12 villages are scattered over the Reservation. Since our filming in the village Ipakup Ekatup Hap ,now called Nova Esperança in Portuguese, we have made friends with the tribe and can offer stays from 4-8 days during which the performance of the rite and the with the bullet ant Paraponera clavata is the highlight. In company of the Sateré we will discover other secrets of the Amazon and the Sateré which is their tradition of cultivation and harvest of Paraponera clavata, the Guaraná plant, a high grade stimulant. Since exposure to various oncogenic stresses activates p53, leading to cell cycle arrest or apoptosis, the turn-on process of p53 is a key step in maintaining genomic integrity, and preventing cells from transforming into cancer cells ([@b4-bmb-50-373]--[@b7-bmb-50-373]). Since the transcriptional role of p53 is to keep cells from growing unchecked under DNA damaging conditions, the toxicity of p53 should be suppressed under normal conditions. This is accomplished by the human double minute 2 (Hdm2, or Mdm2 in mice) E3 ligase, which continually induces the degradation of p53 ([@b5-bmb-50-373], [@b6-bmb-50-373], [@b8-bmb-50-373]). The Jun activation-domain binding protein 1 (Jab1) is also called CSN5, being a fifth member of COP9 signalosome (CSN) complexes ([@b9-bmb-50-373]). One important function of Jab1 is its ability to mediate nuclear export and/or degradation of its interacting proteins. texts.full_text: It is also eaten in the Isan region, an area of Thailand where the majority of the population is of the Lao ethnicity, and among the Hmong people, an ethnic minority group in Laos and Thailand. Local variants of larb also feature in the cuisines of the Tai peoples of Shan State, Burma, and Yunnan province, China. Types Lao/Isan style Larb is most often made with chicken, beef, duck, fish, pork or mushrooms, flavored with fish sauce, lime juice, padaek, roasted ground rice and fresh herbs. The meat can be either raw or cooked; it is minced and mixed with chili, mint and, optionally, assorted vegetables. Roughly ground toasted rice (khao khoua) is also a very important component of the dish. The dish is served at room temperature and usually with a serving of sticky rice and raw or fresh vegetables. However, there are a few tips that might help you speed up the process: 1. Pin Frequently Used References: If you use certain assemblies very often, you can create a shortcut to them by copying them to a specific directory and then adding that directory to the Reference Paths in the project properties. This way, you can find them more quickly in the dialog. 2. Use the Search Bar: The Add Reference dialog has a search bar that can help you find specific assemblies more quickly. Start typing the name of the assembly you need, and the list will be filtered to show only matching references. 3. Customize the Reference Paths: You can add custom paths to the Reference Paths in the project properties. This can help you find assemblies that are not in the default locations more quickly. To do this, right-click on your project in the Solution Explorer, select Properties, go to the Reference Paths tab, and add the paths where you keep your assemblies. 4. Use the Object Browser: If you know the namespace or the type you are looking for, you can use the Object Browser (View > Object Browser) to find the assembly. Once you find the assembly, you can add it to your project. 5. Use Extensions or Macros: While Visual Studio 2008 doesn't support extensions like later versions, you can use macros to automate repetitive tasks. texts.full_text: specifically relates to an insert for a finger hole in the bowling ball. 2. The Prior Art When a mass-produced bowling ball is picked up by a player, it is necessary for the player to squeeze the gripping surface, which is smooth and has a relatively low coefficient of friction. The player may experience some apprehension that the ball will slip from his grip, particularly during his delivery, and deliveries can result in fatigue of the muscles of the hand that grips the ball. If the gripping surface were of a softer and more resilient material with a higher coefficient of friction, it would not be necessary for the player to grip the ball so tightly. This and considerations have led bowlers to experiment with various ways of modifying the grip holes in the ball. Generally, however, when material is removed from the walls of the finger grips, the ball tends to become unbalanced. The American Bowling Congress requires for sanctioned play that the balance of the ball must be within certain specifications, which are relatively stringent. Therefore, when the finger grip holes of a bowling ball are enlarged to accept an insert, care must be taken that the balance of the ball is not disturbed unacceptably. A number of inventors have worked on this problem, and their patents wil: l provide some insight into how they approached it. One such patent was issued in 1975 by Robert Jensen (U.S.A.), which describes a finger grip insert for bowling balls that is made from rubber or other resilient material with coefficient of friction. 3. The Present Invention The present invention provides an improved method and apparatus to modify the gripping surface on mass-produced, commercially available bowling ball without disturbing its balance. 4. Since preliminary studies have suggested that monkeys under stress show an increase in catecholamines as do humans, with a subsequent glucose release from the liver not directly related to insulin deficiency but rather due to alpha-adrenergic stimulation, daily 24-hour urine collections will be done on mother and fetus for catecholamine excretion. texts.full_text: f the Ariane family of rockets, and was produced by Aérospatiale in France. The Ariane 2 is similar to Ariane 3, without additional solid rocket boosters. Its payload capacity was increased to to a geosynchronous transfer orbit. Launch history Ariane 2 flew on 31 May 1986 carrying the Intelsat-5A F-14 satellite. The third stage had a partial ignition followed by another ignition above nominal pressure which led to the engine failure. As a result, the spacecraft was placed into an incorrect orbit. This marked the second failure of the Ariane 2 rocket. The first launch of Ariane 2 occurred on 23 October 1986, carrying the Syncom 4-3 satellite. This launch was unsuccessful, and Syncom 4-3 was placed into the intended geosynchronous transfer orbit. The second launch took place on 16 October 1987, deploying the Intelsat 5A F-15 satellite. This launch was also successful, with the spacecraft reaching the correct orbit. Ariane 2's second launch on 15 October 1987 was intended to place the Aussat 1 satellite into orbit. Unfortunately, the spacecraft failed to achieve the correct orbit due to a failure in the guidance system, resulting in a failure. The first launch on 8 October 1988 carried the Eutelsat 1 F1 satellite. This launch was successful, and the spacecraft was deployed into the correct orbit. The first launch of Ariane 2 occurred on 13 December 1989, deploying the Eutelsat 1 F2 satellite. This launch was not successful, with the spacecraft being inserted into the intended orbit. The Ariane 2 program, despite its initial failures, demonstrated substantial improvements over its predecessor, the Ariane 1, and paved the way for the more successful Ariane 3

By integrating microfabrication technologies like silicon etching, these systems can achieve high precision measurements of various gases with minimal sample preparation. One key advantage is the increased throughput in terms of speedy results that allow real-time decision-making processes which are critical when dealing with volatile compounds or time-sensitive situations. The compact design also makes it feasible for use in remote locations where transportation and installation costs would be prohibitively high if traditional larger systems were employed. However, there's a constant pursuit to improve the performance metrics of these micro-systems by optimizing their separation efficiency without compromising on speedy analysiS capabilities or miniaturization. This involves advancements like novel column materials with better thermal stability for higher operating temperatures and thus improved resolution; more efficient heaters that can rapidly reach high temperature while maintaining uniformity across channels to prevent peak broadening, enhancing the chromatographic performance. Another area of innovation is in detection technologies where microfabricated sensors are being developed. texts.full_text: iations and individual print, broadcast, and online news organizations. While various codes may have some differences, most share common elements including the principles of truthfulness, accuracy, objectivity, impartiality, fairness, and public accountability, as these apply to the acquisition of newsworthy information and its subsequent dissemination to the public. Like many broader ethical systems, the ethics of journalism include the principle of "limitation of harm." This may involve the withholding of certain details from reports, such as the names of minor children, crime victims' names, or information not materially related to the news report where the release of such information might, for example, harm someone's reputation. Some journalistic codes of ethics, notably some European codes, also include a concern with discriminatory references in news based on race, religion, sexual orientation, and physical or mental disabilities. The Parliamentary AssemblyThe Parliamentary Assembly, particularly the Council of Europe’s Parliamentary Assembly, has historically engaged with media ethics, especially in fostering democratic values and human rights. Its recommendations and reports often stress the importance of ethical journalism in maintaining democratic societies, emphasizing the role of journalists as guardians of transparency and public trust. Below is an expanded overview of journalism ethics and standards, incorporating these elements: --- ### Core Principles of Journalism Ethics 1. Truthfulness and Accuracy: - Journalists must verify facts rigorously before publication. Errors should be corrected promptly and transparently. - Misrepresentation or manipulation of facts undermines public trust. 2. Objectivity and Impartiality: - Reports should avoid bias, presenting multiple perspectives to allow readers to form their own conclusions. - However, this principle has been challenged in recent years, as some argue that "neutrality" can perpetuate systemic injustices. Contextual fairness (highlighting marginalized voices) is increasingly emphasized. 3. Fairness and Equity: - Give subjects of news the opportunity to respond to allegations. - Avoid stereotyping or discriminatory language, as mandated by codes like the European Code of Journalistic Conduct. This includes respecting privacy, especially for vulnerable groups (e.g., children, crime victims). 4. Public Accountability: - Journalism serves the public good. This includes exposing corruption and holding power to account, even when it incurs backlash.

Here’s a few photos from Sakunami Highway that leads to the distillery: See this photo on Flickr See this photo on Flickr The mountain above is called Kamakurayama. I read later that it is possible to go hiking there from the hot springs, so it’s a shame we missed that opportunity. See this photo on Flickr The distillery is just around the corner in the image above. Despite all the bugs it was a pleasant 20-minute walk to the distillery and although we spent a good amount of time goofing around and taking photos of the insects and other stuff, we managed to get there well before the bus.
